items.title: Disagreement without flattery is the upgrade that matters clinically
items.body: NLW's standout was a model that disagreed with him and then updated on new evidence rather than telling him what he wanted to hear. For any clinical decision support that property matters far more than raw capability: a tool that sycophantically confirms your working diagnosis is dangerous. It is worth testing any clinical AI the same way — feed it a deliberately wrong steer and see whether it pushes back or simply agrees.

items.title: "Task imagination" is interesting; bounded reliability is what a surgery needs
items.body: The episode urges us to hand agents days of responsibility, not minutes of tasks. In a consulting room the relevant test is narrower: does the tool reliably do one bounded job — turning a ten-minute consultation into an accurate, editable note? If NLW is right that the frontier has leapt, scribes will quietly absorb more (coding, referral drafting); if Zitron is right that the output is impressive-looking but unreliable, the risk is exactly the long-running autonomy he warns about. Either way the discipline is unchanged: read every word before it enters the record.

items.title: Usage-based pricing is the lab's problem becoming the practice's
items.body: The real-world thread here is that token billing makes cost unpredictable. Practices on fixed per-seat scribe contracts are insulated for now, but the labs have moved to usage pricing and vendors may follow. Worth checking whether your scribe contract is a fixed per-seat fee or exposed to token-based pricing, and what happens at renewal.
